steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Boundaries question. The blank connects two independent clauses: "many are still well [blank]" and "Malika Ouattara, for example, is a celebrated slam poet from Burkina Faso." The second clause is an example that illustrates the first. The correct punctuation must join two independent clauses without creating a comma splice or run-on, while signaling that an example follows.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — known,: Placing only a comma between two independent clauses creates a comma splice, a boundary error. ✗
  • Choice B — known:: A colon after a complete independent clause correctly introduces an example or elaboration. "Many are still well known" is a complete independent clause, and what follows is a concrete example of that claim. ✓
  • Choice C — known and: The coordinating conjunction "and" would need a comma before it ("known, and") to join two independent clauses, and even then it implies addition rather than an example relationship signaled by "for example." Without the comma, it is also a boundary error. ✗
  • Choice D — known: No punctuation between two independent clauses creates a run-on sentence. ✗
That matches Choice B — known:.
